text.fullText: Board of Directors Doug Crow Ghost, SRST Chairman Reno Red Cloud, OST Vice Chairman Issabelle Provost, YST Secretary Mary J. Gourneau, LBST Treasurer Elizabeth Wakeman, FSST Sargeant at Arms Syed Huq, RST Member Robert “Bob” Walters, CRST Member Submitted via https://www.regulations.gov February 17, 2026 U.S Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington D.C. 20460 RE: Docket ID. No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Comment on proposed rule – Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations To the Environmental Protection Agency: I serve as Chairman of the Board of Directors of the Great Plains Tribal Water Alliance, Inc. The Tribal Water Alliance is chartered for the purposes of protecting the water rights of the Great Plains Tribes, reserved in our Treaties with the United States, and for advising the Great Plains Tribal Chairman’s Association on projects and policies affecting our water. The Board of Directors is comprised of representatives from the Standing Rock, Rosebud, Oglala, Lower Brule, Cheyenne River, Yankton and Flandreau Santee Sioux Tribes. I write on behalf of the Board, to express our strong opposition to the proposed rule diminishing the role of Tribes and other certifying authorities to protect our water under section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Our comments focus on three areas: (1) The proposed rule on water quality certification is part of a broader agenda to diminish protections for water; (2) EPA failed to engage in Nation-to-Nation Consultation; and (3) The proposed rule diminishes Tribal sovereignty and Transparency in Decision-making under the Clean Water Act. ____________________________________________________________________________________ P.O. Box 271 www.tribalwateralliance.org Pine Ridge, SD 57770 Letter to Environmental Protection Agency Docket ID. No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 February 17, 2026 Page Two The Proposed Rule is Part of a Broader Agenda to Diminish Protections for Water The Tribal Water Alliance is concerned with the proposed rule, taken in conjunction with other administrative actions by EPA. The proposed re-definition of “navigable waters,” also referred to as “waters of the United States,” will remove protections under the Clean Water Act to millions of acres of wetlands and important ephemeral waters. 90 Fed. Reg. 52498 (Nov. 20, 2025). The waters losing their protections under the revised definition produce valuable habitat for waterfowl and wildlife, and remove carbon from the atmosphere, at a time when greenhouse gas emissions are affecting our planet and our fresh water. On February 12, EPA announced a final rule rescinding the greenhouse gas emissions endangerment finding, which established the basis for the regulation of greenhouse gas emissions and motor vehicle standards under the Clean Air Act. https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions- vehicles-and-engines/final-rule-rescission-greenhouse-gas-endangerment. The proliferation of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere has caused persistent drought in the northern plains, enhancing the need to protect our valuable water. The proposed rule does the opposite. It would strip from Tribes and other certifying authorities our ability to influence decisions that affect the quality of our water. The multitude of proposed Clean Water and Clean Air Act rollbacks is staggering and poses severe implications for the Great Plains Tribes. EPA Failed to Engage in Nation-to-Nation Consultation For the Great Plains Tribes, Nation-to-Nation consultation is a Treaty right under Article 5 of the Fort Laramie Treaty of 1868. 15 Stat. 635. This provision states in part: The United States agrees that the agent for said Indians shall… (conduct) prompt and diligent inquiry into such matters of complaint by and against the Indians as may be presented for investigation under the provisions of their Treaty stipulations. 15 Stat. 636. This Treaty right is exercised today pursuant to Executive Order 13175 on Consultation and Coordination with Indian Tribal Governments. This executive order establishes the framework for the Nation-to-Nation relationship between the executive branch and the Indian Nations. Under E.O. 13175: ____________________________________________________________________________________ P.O. Box 271 www.tribalwateralliance.org Pine Ridge, SD 57770 Letter to Environmental Protection Agency Docket ID. No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 February 17, 2026 Page Three The United States continues to work with Indian tribes on government-to- government basis to address issues concerning tribal self government, tribal trust resources, and Indian tribal treaty and other rights…The United States recognizes the right of Indian tribes to self government and supports tribal sovereignty and self determination. Each agency shall have an accountable process to ensure meaningful and timely input by tribal officials in the development of regulatory policies that have tribal implications. 65 Fed. Reg. 67249-67250. Agencies such as EPA must promulgate their own policies to implement E.O. 13175. The EPA has issued its EPA Policy on Consultation with Indian Tribes (2023). According to the policy, “[C]onsultation meetings are government-to-government exchanges of information and dialogue between official representatives of the EPA and of federally-recognizes Tribal governments.” EPA Policy on Consultation with Indian Tribes, p. 5. The policy explicitly requires consultation prior to the issuance of “[r]egulations or rules.” Id. In the development of the proposed rule, EPA conducted no “prompt and diligent inquiry” into the concerns of the Great Plains Tribes on implementation of Clean Water Act section 401. 15 Stat. 636. There was no opportunity for “meaningful and timely input” by the Great Plains Tribes, as required in E.O. 13175. 65 Fed. Reg. 67249. Similarly, there were no “government-to- government exchanges of dialogue” in consultation meetings as required in the EPA Policy. https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2025-04/epa-policy-on-consultation-with-indian- tribes.pdf. Thus, EPA is violating article 5 of the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty, E.O. 13175 and the EPA Policy. A form letter, webinar and inter-Tribal staff level meetings do not constitute Nation-to- Nation consultation. 91 Fed. Reg. 2039. EPA’s failure to properly engage in the required Nation- to-Nation consultation with the Great Plains renders any final rule arbitrary and capricious within the meaning of the Administrative Procedures Act. 5 U.S.C. §706(2)(A). Moreover, the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples requires the Tribes’ free, prior and informed consent to this diminishment of protections for Tribal waters. Consent has not been given. No final rule should be issued. ____________________________________________________________________________________ P.O. Box 271 www.tribalwateralliance.org Pine Ridge, SD 57770 Letter to Environmental Protection Agency Docket ID. No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 February 17, 2026 Page Four The Proposed Rule Diminishes Tribal Sovereignty and Transparency in Decision-making The proposed rule diminishes the Tribal role in implementing section 401 and undermines transparency in environmental decision-making. It eliminates the “additional contents” for applications currently required in 40 CFR §121.5(b)(5) & (6), thereby diminishing the available information for local decision-makers. The proposed rule also eliminates the requirement to analyze the “activity as a whole,” and requires Tribes to look at “discharge only.” That eliminates information needed for environmental decision-making, and violates Tribal sovereignty, the Clean Water Act itself and EPA’s mission to protect public health. In the preamble to the proposed rule, EPA endorses government-in-secrecy: [T]he proposed rule would limit any such materials to “water quality- related materials on any potential discharges.” Accordingly, applicants may redact or exclude… sensitive information…. The EPA acknowledges the desire of certifying authorities to have all necessary information as soon as possible in the certification process, but the Agency must balance that desire against the need for… certainty regarding the required contents of a request for certification. 91 Fed. Reg. 2019-2020. The proposed limits to the authority of Tribes (and other certifying authorities) to require information about a project’s environmental effects are highly problematic. The regulations should not limit the information to be made available to Tribes, or the authority of Tribes to certify projects based on their overall effect. Information should not be limited to “discharge only.” Sections 121.5(b) and (c) are important regulations for transparency and to enable Tribal leaders and the public to learn the overall environmental effects of proposed projects. EPA should not make any changes to these regulations. The only regulatory uncertainty was caused by EPA itself, when it issued the 2020 rule, upending regulations that had been in place for 50 years. The current proposed rule will cause uncertainty, by limiting the ability of Tribes and other certifying authorities to obtain information needed to determine the water quality impacts of proposed projects. It will also create confusion about timeframes for the approval or denial of certification, when applicants fail to provide sufficient information. That will result in needless litigation, which could further stress the Tribal role under section 401 of the Clean Water Act. ____________________________________________________________________________________ P.O. Box 271 www.tribalwateralliance.org Pine Ridge, SD 57770 Letter to Environmental Protection Agency Docket ID. No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 February 17, 2026 Page Five The proposed rule will diminish the opportunity for Tribes to certify whether federally- approved projects will impact the quality of Tribal waters. Indian water rights arise from our Treaties with the United States. The diminishment of protections for our water in the proposed rule violates the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe’s rights under the 1851 and 1868 Fort Laramie Treaties, as well as the Tribe’s role as prescribed in the Clean Water Act. There should be no changes to the TAS procedures in the absence of consultation and collaboration with Tribal leadership – which has not occurred. EPA has failed to explain in the preamble any rationale for the radical departure to the 2023 rule or to longstanding EPA policy in the implementation of Clean Water Act section 401 – in violation of the Administrative Procedures Act. Ultimately, the proposed rule violates Tribal sovereignty and our Treaty-guaranteed water rights, and is inconsistent with the Clean Water Act, which delineates a strong role for Tribes in decisions affecting Tribal waters. 33 U.S.C. §§1251(b), 1377. For these reasons, we oppose the proposed rule, and insist, in the strongest possible terms, that EPA abandon this rulemaking and get back to its mission of protecting public health and the environment, including our sacred water. Sincerely, Erroll “Doug” Crow Ghost, Jr., Chairman, Board of Directors Cc: Gay Kingman, Executive Director Great Plains Tribal Chairmen’s Association ____________________________________________________________________________________ P.O. Box 271 www.tribalwateralliance.org Pine Ridge, SD 57770
